title: Why People in The Coldest Places Wear Yellow Mittens.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: The humble mitten. One of the objects humanity has really not improved for forever.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: These are some of the oldest intact mittens ever found. They belong to a child in Iceland around the year 900.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: You can tell they were for a child because even 1100 years ago parents were like, "Okay, you lost the mittens? Not a- Nope, mommy's not mad at all. You used your super sight and couldn't find them? And you- okay."

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Today, we are focused on two of the most famous forms of mittens ever created: the yellow mitten and the ice mitten, for lack of a better term.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: We shall begin by testing one of them.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Ambient temperature outside, 30 degrees on the dot. But for today's purposes, we care specifically about the temperature of the water.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Okay, small problem with our experiment. I am currently standing on the river that I wanted to test the temperature of.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Drat. Oh, okay, that's- We'll find other water. I think I saw a little puddle somewhere else.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Stupid, stupid moron Michael didn't think the river- It's just simple math if the temperature's not- You had such a good idea before you checked the temperature in the morning before you went outside.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Ambient temperature outside, 30 degrees. Temperature of the water: 34.2 degrees Fahrenheit, 1.2 degrees Celsius. Okay, not as climactic as I envisioned, but here we go.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: These are Dachstein mittens, loved by ice climbers because they're extremely warm, wind resistant, water resistant, and flexible.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Some climbers say they don't get good until they get an ice skin on them from climbing all day. Fishermen also did something very similar with their mittens.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Yeah! New England fishermen's wives would knit their husbands a pair of mittens that were about 30 percent too big for their hands.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Then every single day that fisherman on his way steaming out would get his mittens soaking wet in warm water and stomp on them on the deck until they shrunk down to the correct size.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: I'm trying to do that with these right now. Hopefully you could see the size difference. Felted wool mittens are some of the thickest, warmest wool mittens that you could possibly make.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But they're not the warmest wool mittens you can make.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: The warmest knit wool mittens probably came in the 19th century in the Adirondacks, Maine, Massachusetts, or Vermont. They were called buff mittens or shag mittens.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Very, very cool. There's not a lot of information about them. There's sparse pictures and paintings that show a buff mitten on the ground or something like that. They're in museums, there's stuff like that about them. And I have a pair.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: I found them on eBay for a hundred bucks and I bought them and I'm going to donate them to a museum. How cool is that?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: That is one type of extremely famous super popular mitten that you see everywhere. But there's another type of mitten that is even more popular.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: That is very, very famous in Minnesota, in Wisconsin, in South Dakota, in Maine, that I am going to put on my hand right now. But the thumb is a little mispositioned.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: The extremely famous and legendary yellow mitten. What's so great about it? Why does everybody in Minnesota and Canada and all the really cold places wear it? And why is it so warm?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Those two styles of mitten set the formula for mittens forever. They've never changed. This style of mitten is around 300 years old.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And why haven't they changed? Well, 300 years ago something happened that basically made-

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So, the perfect mitten was figured out. Before that, there were two different ones, and then there was one. And so, we will be splitting the perfect mitten up into its two halves and dissecting each one. I broke this video into three easy-to-digest parts.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Who put the yellow in these yellow mittens? I'm wearing two different mittens for very important reasons. This is the gold standard in yellow mitten material. This is the modern version. We use this modern version because it's a lot cheaper and almost as effective, but it's not as good as the original. As you could probably see, the leather between the two looks very different.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Indigenous people in northern areas wrote the rule book on leather mittens. We have the Inuit, the Yupik, the Sami, Alaskan Native, uh, Cree, goodness there's quite a lot of tribes that really contributed to the world of mittens.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: They wrote the rule book on what makes a good mitten because they did not change quality for big machines or anything like that, so their stuff was insane.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: If we start with the Inuit people, they are masters at making a double-layered mitten called the poaluk. That's a very important mitten with seal skin on the outside, caribou hide on the inside with the fur still on, facing your hand.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: The Inuit people, because of where they lived and the materials they had around them, had to process leather in a very, very specific and intensive way. They were scraping fat and blubber and everything off from seals.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: They had to actually chew the leather to make it soft enough to work with. Obviously, that got superseded by machines that now use metal teeth to mimic the same thing going on.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But the thing to note is that they got their insulation from the actual leather itself. They left the hair on the caribou to keep their hands warm. They didn't put something else in the mittens on those double mittens.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Okay, say it with me one more time, Odoo.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Odoo.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Nope, that's still not it. Slow-mo Michael, say it with me.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Odoo.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Okay, perfect. What does it do?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Um, well, it's an open source suite for business management across multiple applications.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Okay, overachiever Michael, you go.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Well, with Odoo, you can do quite a lot. You can make and manage a website for your business, track inventory, manage sales, email marketing, timesheets... the world is really your...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Boom! Okay, concise Michael, sum it up.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Uh, okay. It is all of your business needs on one platform, but it's broken up into easy-to-manage apps for each need.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Good job, I like that. Anxious Michael, how should we use it?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Um, okay, I wrote down a stupid idea. Odoo's easy-to-use and very fast website builder could come in handy, especially because the first app you choose from Odoo is free forever for unlimited users. Isn't that great? Just type in what you need, select the modules, and boom, there you go. Your website's done and the domain is free for the first year. I'm sorry I messed up a little earlier in that sentence.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: No, that was actually a perfectly valid idea. (Claps) Passive-aggressive Michael, bring us home.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Uh, jeez, you're just so successful...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Like we could do anything. Let's say that YouTube clothing brand thing you're doing, the Iron Sag, is doing really well, maybe you need an inventory management software to keep track of your big heavy animal-themed jackets.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Perfect, great idea. Thank you very much Odoo for sponsoring this video. More information is in the link down below.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Now if we go south from that and we focus on the Anishinaabe people, which by the way if I say something wrong or something like that, I researched, practiced pronunciation as hard as I possibly could, not perfect. Sorry if there's a mistake.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: The Anishinaabe people and tribes around the Great Lakes focused on buckskin, deer-skin, moose, elk, which all of those are premiere glove leather.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Like this right here, this is buckskin.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: The characteristics of buckskin and elk and moose and everything combined with the way it was processed make it extremely soft, very stretchy, silent when you're moving around so if you're hunting it doesn't creak.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Breathable, easy to repair because the leather is so soft, it was easy to pierce, and most importantly in the winter, well I guess in general, if it got wet it didn't stiffen up over time, so it always stayed soft, pliable, and stretchy. It's really great for what you needed.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: All of these hides that were tanned and processed in the traditional way were legendary because of all the work it actually took to make it. You had to agitate the leather as it was tanning, then you had to put it over smoke so that way it didn't stiffen up and harden because it broke down all the fibers inside so they didn't glue together.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: It took a lot of time, and because of the characteristics of buckskin, moose, and elk, it also took an expert to say, 'Okay, this leather is a little stretchier here, this one is soft like this, this one's a little dryer so I need to process it this way exactly.' But it paid off in spades.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: All of these leathers are yellow because of the smoking process that I talked about before. When the leather was first processed, it was basically white and then the smoke turned it this color.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And I'm assuming by the time that these were sold in places like Sears and stuff like that, with the first version being seen in 1902, it could have been sold before that but there's not a lot of records of it. By that time people associated the color yellow with buckskin mittens and good quality, so manufacturers dyed their stuff yellow to match.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: I would assume.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Obviously now in 2026 with modern tanning processes and machines that can agitate stuff and things like that, usually the gap is almost closed. Buckskin is still probably a superior leather, but cowhide is way cheaper, way easier to get, way easier to work with, and this is soft, treated to be very water resistant, tough, durable, and more consistent throughout.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Pigskin is also another alternative that is used that is very soft, has similar qualities to buckskin and stuff like that, but obviously way cheaper because a lot more people eat pork and stuff like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So the yellow mitten was considered to be the gold standard of quality, but the reason that I mentioned the Falu is because of the insulation that goes inside of the mitten.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: We have the gold standard of the outside of the mitten, but the inside's not so easy. I actually, I shouldn't put it like that. There were many, many, many solutions. You could stuff the mittens with grasses or buffalo down, like the undercoat of a buffalo, which is incredibly warm, or you could just use animal hides and furs themselves to keep your hands warm.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But if you combine the perfect outer with the perfect inner...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Men outer with a specific little animal that bounces around, in some cases eats kelp, in other cases eats alfalfa, in other things you get kind of the world's most loved mitten, the mitten that everybody likes, the yellow most famous Minnesota mitten. I'm saying Minnesota obviously we'll get, it's not just Minnesota but we'll get there in a second.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: People that had sheep absolutely loved sheep and while of course everybody was kind of working on leather at the same time if you had sheep available to you you were like let's not focus solely on leather let's put a lot of thought into this.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: People that have a ton of sheep's wool are figuring out how could we develop the best strongest most durable mittens possible and people with a ton of leather are figuring out the same thing but with their materials that are more readily accessible to them. But they both have downsides.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Naturally insulating, it's warm immediately, you don't need to add anything else. Also very good when it's wet, it keeps your hand warm, but if you're swinging an axe or doing crazy work, not very durable so you need to keep remaking these all of the time.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: On the other hand we have something that is not naturally insulated but very very durable, windproof, not so good in the water but still doesn't creak and freeze up if it gets wet.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So now if we fast forward to the 1700s and the 1800s in the US specifically, similar stuff was actually happening around the world, the Sami people were combining their reindeer outers with knit inners, but we have a massive logging industry in the US where people are swinging axes all day long. They need something to protect their hands and keep them warm. We also have knitting everywhere. It's cheap, it's fast, you can do it at home, you can make mittens all day long.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So we make an over mitten like the Pualu but with different materials. This helps you grip an axe, protects your hands, also you can take this off or if a machine grabs your hand this comes off and you still have this mitten underneath it so if you need to vent your hands during the day but not freeze them off you have wool. And at the end of the day you take the wool mittens you put them by the stove they dry off for the next day or you rotate the mittens out.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So what do you call your mittens? Choppers. This is a chopper mitten. These are choppers, L.L. Bean ones are choppers, technically the M-1949 US Army extreme cold mittens, choppers.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: We'll look at how insanely far we have come in leather mitten technology. We have strategic leather reinforcements all over the mitten so you can hold ski poles. We have this lobster claw that was originally made so you could shoot a gun easier. Neoprene cuffs, really cool applied logo things.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But through all of that and with all of that at the end of the day we're still just following that same formula of wool or synthetic material nowadays and leather. 300 years we haven't come up with something that's better for most people which is crazy.

highlights: The "chopper" mitten follows a 300-year-old design formula that combines a windproof, durable leather outer shell with a warm wool inner liner to balance protection and insulation.

Indigenous peoples, such as the Inuit and Anishinaabe, established the standards for cold-weather handwear by utilizing materials like caribou hide with the fur intact and smoke-tanned buckskin that remains soft even after getting wet.
The iconic yellow color of modern work mittens is a legacy of the traditional smoke-tanning process used for buckskin, which manufacturers eventually began to mimic with dyes to signify high quality.

Historical specialized designs, such as the felted wool mittens favored by New England fishermen and the rare 19th-century "shag" mittens, provided extreme warmth through thickness and unique shrinking techniques.
summary:
This transcript explores the history and functional evolution of the mitten, noting that its fundamental design has remained largely unchanged for over a millennium. The discussion centers on two primary styles: high-density felted wool mittens, such as the Dachstein and historical "buff" mittens, and the legendary "yellow" leather mittens. The author emphasizes that modern cold-weather gear owes its effectiveness to Indigenous technologies from the Inuit and Anishinaabe peoples, who perfected the use of smoked buckskin and double-layered animal hides to create handwear that was durable, flexible, and insulating even when wet.

A key theme is the development of "choppers," a style of mitten that became the gold standard during the 18th and 19th-century North American logging boom. By marrying a rugged leather outer shell with a removable wool knit liner, this design provided the durability needed for heavy labor alongside the superior insulation of wool. The transcript concludes that while modern materials like neoprene and strategic reinforcements have been introduced, the core formula of a protective outer layer and an insulating inner layer has remained the most effective solution for extreme cold for over 300 years.
description:
Thanks, Odoo! All your business needs on ONE platform. https://www.odoo.com/r/gFC

Mittens! It's February!! Michael!! You moron!! 

Hey! That isn't nice! Don't say things like that. But yeah, whoops. Little backed up over here at Snail HQ. What can you do? Maybe next year you can toss some of these puppies on your puppies. Get those fingies all nice and toasty? Little warm sausages. Anyway, today we are looking at some of the greatest mittens on the planet, namely, the Chopper mitten. 

Mittens in this video: 

Dachstein: https://www.sweaterchalet.com/dachstein-woolwear/dw-3112-adult-mitts/#cc-m-product-11727270031
Frost River: https://frostriver.com/products/pennsylvania-choppers-waxed-canvas
L.L. Bean: https://www.llbean.com/llb/shop/60315?page=mens-buckskin-chopper-mitts
M-49 Mittens (surplus)
Buff Mittens (vintage/not produced anymore)
Hestra: https://www.hestragloves.us/fall-line-3-finger-cork-cork
Raber Garbage Mitten: https://cleanflow.net/products/raber-344-mens-pile-lined-leather-garbage-mitts-genuine-cowhide-warm-insulation-made-in-winnipeg-canada-for-extreme-cold-weather?srsltid=AfmBOorEANg-NhBvPrEq7SwJ5iBz3eQM7eXIe0Kj6mAq5S-dhbbZ0dMi
Kincos: https://www.kinco.com/1927kwt-l
Two Discontinued Filson Mittens

Can I interest you in a hot bowl of following ME and The Snail in other places??

 / theironsnail

 / @michaelkristy1868

#theironsnail

The Iron Snail is a men’s fashion vlog starring a young man named Michael and featuring a snail no bigger than a quarter. The two are set on taking over the world of fashion by creating a clothing line to end all clothing lines. Until then, we’re here to tell you EVERYTHING you need to know about the best clothing out there, from the highest quality raw denim jeans to the warmest jackets to the sturdiest boots…the Iron Snail has got you covered. (written by Taylor, though Michael is sometimes known to talk about himself in the third person… xoxo)
topics: History and evolution of famous mitten styles
Indigenous leather-making and mitten construction traditions
The design and practical utility of Chopper mittens
Comparison of leather and insulation materials for cold-weather gear
Business management and website building with Odoo
